seo.title: Moving to the Netherlands from France | Registration, Documents, Costs & Practical Setup
seo.description: Planning a move from France to the Netherlands? Explore EU move rules, registration, BSN, document requirements, housing, banking, and the practical steps to settle in.
keywords: moving to the netherlands from france
eu citizen move to netherlands from france
living in the netherlands as a french citizen

faqOverrides.q: Do French citizens need a visa to live in the Netherlands?
faqOverrides.a: French nationals are EU citizens. For ordinary long-term residence based on EU free movement, you generally do not need a visa or standard residence permit in the same way as non-EU nationals. You must still meet the conditions that apply to your situation. Confirm on the IND page for EU, EEA, and Swiss citizens and on Government.nl.

faqOverrides.q: Do I need an MVV to move from France to the Netherlands?
faqOverrides.a: An MVV is used in many non-EU long-stay procedures. It is not the standard framework for French nationals exercising EU free movement for ordinary residence. Unusual cases may still need tailored checks—use official sources or professional advice if unsure.

faqOverrides.q: How do I register after moving from France?
faqOverrides.a: If you will stay in the Netherlands for more than four months, you normally register in the BRP through your municipality. Government.nl states you must register within five days of arrival. The municipality issues your BSN. See our municipality registration guide and the Government.nl BRP pages.

faqOverrides.q: Do French documents need translation for the Netherlands?
faqOverrides.a: Netherlands Worldwide indicates that foreign official documents in French do not need to be translated for use in the Netherlands. Some documents can be used immediately; others may need an apostille from the French authorities. Always confirm for your specific document and the organisation that requests it.

faqOverrides.q: What should I prioritise in the first weeks?
faqOverrides.a: Most people focus on BRP registration and BSN, Dutch basic health insurance where required, a bank account, and DigiD. Housing search intensity is often the hardest part, not immigration paperwork.

faqOverrides.q: When might immigration lawyers or visa consultants still help?
faqOverrides.a: Complex family situations, verification against EU law, or specialised legal questions may warrant support. For typical EU moves, many people start with gemeente guidance and official web pages.

customNotes: EU movers from France often prioritise housing platforms and banking after registration planning.
